verses.text: Now it came to pass after the death of Saul, when David was returned from the slaughter of the Amalekites, and David had abode two days in Ziklag; It came even to pass on the third day, that, behold, a man came out of the camp from Saul with his clothes rent, and earth upon his head: and so it was, when he came to David, that he fell to the earth, and did obeisance. Guidance: 2 Samuel opens at the seam between two reigns. David has been back at Ziklag two days, presumably putting out fires from the Amalekite raid — repairing the burned town, redistributing the recovered spoil, restoring his household. He does not yet know what has happened on Gilboa. The third day, a man arrives from the north. The signs of mourning are precise: clothes rent (the standard Hebrew gesture of grief, tearing the outer garment from the neck), earth on the head (dust scattered over the hair as a sign of death-news). He prostrates. Everything in his approach is calibrated to communicate: I bring catastrophic news. The chapter is about to test David in a way the wilderness years never did. The temptation he is going to face will not be a spear or an ambush; it is going to be a piece of looted royal jewelry, presented by a man who thinks David will reward the bearer.

Now it came to pass after the death of Saul, when David was returned from the slaughter of the Amalekites, and David had abode two days in Ziklag; It came even to pass on the third day, that, behold, a man came out of the camp from Saul with his clothes rent, and earth upon his head: and so it was, when he came to David, that he fell to the earth, and did obeisance. Guidance: 2 Samuel opens at the seam between two reigns. David has been back at Ziklag two days, presumably putting out fires from the Amalekite raid — repairing the burned town, redistributing the recovered spoil, restoring his household. He does not yet know what has happened on Gilboa. The third day, a man arrives from the north. The signs of mourning are precise: clothes rent (the standard Hebrew gesture of grief, tearing the outer garment from the neck), earth on the head (dust scattered over the hair as a sign of death-news). He prostrates. Everything in his approach is calibrated to communicate: I bring catastrophic news. The chapter is about to test David in a way the wilderness years never did. The temptation he is going to face will not be a spear or an ambush; it is going to be a piece of looted royal jewelry, presented by a man who thinks David will reward the bearer.

And David said unto him, From whence comest thou? And he said unto him, Out of the camp of Israel am I escaped. And David said unto him, How went the matter? I pray thee, tell me. And he answered, That the people are fled from the battle, and many of the people also are fallen and dead; and Saul and Jonathan his son are dead also. Guidance: David's first question is location ('whence comest thou?'). The man answers: 'out of the camp of Israel am I escaped.' The narrator's irony is being set up here — the man claims Israelite identity by location, but his ancestry (about to be revealed) is Amalekite. David's second question is reporting ('how went the matter?'). The answer is the news he has been dreading. 'The people are fled from the battle, and many of the people also are fallen and dead; and Saul and Jonathan his son are dead also.' Four bad clauses, descending. Notice the messenger's instinct: he names Saul and Jonathan together — political king and beloved friend, dynastic threat and covenant friend — in the same breath, because he is about to claim credit for the death of one of them. He must know what David's relationship to the two men has been; his lie is calibrated to David's politics. He thinks he is bringing good news from a regime change. He misreads David completely.

And David said unto him, From whence comest thou? And he said unto him, Out of the camp of Israel am I escaped. And David said unto him, How went the matter? I pray thee, tell me. And he answered, That the people are fled from the battle, and many of the people also are fallen and dead; and Saul and Jonathan his son are dead also. Guidance: David's first question is location ('whence comest thou?'). The man answers: 'out of the camp of Israel am I escaped.' The narrator's irony is being set up here — the man claims Israelite identity by location, but his ancestry (about to be revealed) is Amalekite. David's second question is reporting ('how went the matter?'). The answer is the news he has been dreading. 'The people are fled from the battle, and many of the people also are fallen and dead; and Saul and Jonathan his son are dead also.' Four bad clauses, descending. Notice the messenger's instinct: he names Saul and Jonathan together — political king and beloved friend, dynastic threat and covenant friend — in the same breath, because he is about to claim credit for the death of one of them. And David said unto the young man that told him, How knowest thou that Saul and Jonathan his son be dead? And the young man that told him said, As I happened by chance upon mount Gilboa, behold, Saul leaned upon his spear; and, lo, the chariots and horsemen followed hard after him. Guidance: David presses for verification. 'How knowest thou that Saul and Jonathan his son be dead?' The question is sober and judicial; David will not act on unverified news. The young man delivers his account: 'As I happened by chance upon mount Gilboa' — the literary phrase 'happened by chance' is the narrator's quiet flag that something fabricated may be coming. Compare 1 Samuel 31:4-5, where Saul falls on his own sword and his armor-bearer follows him. We know from chapter 31 exactly what happened. The Amalekite is going to tell a different story. The narrator has positioned us as eyewitnesses to the real death, so we can detect the lie as it lands. 'Saul leaned upon his spear; and the chariots and horsemen followed hard after him.' The geography is plausible (post-battle scavenging), the chariots-and-horsemen detail is realistic. But the central claim is wrong. We know Saul was already dead by his own hand before any Amalekite touched him. The young man is going to claim credit for an act he did not perform.

And David said unto the young man that told him, How knowest thou that Saul and Jonathan his son be dead? And the young man that told him said, As I happened by chance upon mount Gilboa, behold, Saul leaned upon his spear; and, lo, the chariots and horsemen followed hard after him. Guidance: David presses for verification. 'How knowest thou that Saul and Jonathan his son be dead?' The question is sober and judicial; David will not act on unverified news. The geography is plausible (post-battle scavenging), the chariots-and-horsemen detail is realistic. But the central claim is wrong. And when he looked behind him, he saw me, and called unto me. And I answered, Here am I. And he said unto me, Who art thou? And I answered him, I am an Amalekite. Guidance: The dialogue inside the story has Saul calling the man, asking his identity. The Amalekite identifies himself by ethnicity. Note: he tells David this voluntarily. He is not hiding what he is. He must assume David, exiled by Saul, fighting Saul's enemies, will be indifferent or even pleased by an Amalekite hand in the king's death. He has no idea that David spent the previous week destroying an Amalekite raiding party that had burned David's own town. The narrator's irony deepens — the messenger is presenting himself as the killer of Saul to David, who is, at this moment, sore from his own war with Amalekites. The identity that the messenger thinks will earn him a reward is the very identity he should have concealed. People who try to flatter the powerful with the wrong fact about themselves are often closer to harm than they realize.

And when he looked behind him, he saw me, and called unto me. And I answered, Here am I. And he said unto me, Who art thou? And I answered him, I am an Amalekite. Guidance: The dialogue inside the story has Saul calling the man, asking his identity. The Amalekite identifies himself by ethnicity. Note: he tells David this voluntarily. He is not hiding what he is. He must assume David, exiled by Saul, fighting Saul's enemies, will be indifferent or even pleased by an Amalekite hand in the king's death. He has no idea that David spent the previous week destroying an Amalekite raiding party that had burned David's own town. The narrator's irony deepens — the messenger is presenting himself as the killer of Saul to David, who is, at this moment, sore from his own war with Amalekites. The identity that the messenger thinks will earn him a reward is the very identity he should have concealed. People who try to flatter the powerful with the wrong fact about themselves are often closer to harm than they realize.

He said unto me again, Stand, I pray thee, upon me, and slay me: for anguish is come upon me, because my life is yet whole in me. So I stood upon him, and slew him, because I was sure that he could not live after that he was fallen: and I took the crown that was upon his head, and the bracelet that was on his arm, and have brought them hither unto my lord. Guidance: The story's punchline. The Amalekite claims he performed the mercy-killing Saul actually asked his armor-bearer for and the armor-bearer refused (1 Sam 31:4). The detail is plausible-sounding — Saul in extremis would have wanted to be put out of misery — but the chapter-31 narrative explicitly says Saul died by his own hand before any other agent touched him. The Amalekite is fabricating. He probably arrived on the field after the battle, found the king already dead, and looted the royal regalia. To explain how he came by them, he invented the mercy-killing. The crown (the royal nezer, a circlet rather than a full-blown crown) and the armband (royal jewelry) are exactly what a scavenger would have taken from a dead king. He brings them to David expecting payment — 'have brought them hither unto my lord.' The 'my lord' is calculated; he has decided David is the next king and wants to be the first to bring the regalia to him. He is half-right about the kingdom transfer and totally wrong about what David will do with the news.

He said unto me again, Stand, I pray thee, upon me, and slay me: for anguish is come upon me, because my life is yet whole in me. So I stood upon him, and slew him, because I was sure that he could not live after that he was fallen: and I took the crown that was upon his head, and the bracelet that was on his arm, and have brought them hither unto my lord. Guidance: The story's punchline. The Amalekite claims he performed the mercy-killing Saul actually asked his armor-bearer for and the armor-bearer refused (1 Sam 31:4). The detail is plausible-sounding — Saul in extremis would have wanted to be put out of misery — but the chapter-31 narrative explicitly says Saul died by his own hand before any other agent touched him. The Amalekite is fabricating. He probably arrived on the field after the battle, found the king already dead, and looted the royal regalia. To explain how he came by them, he invented the mercy-killing. The crown (the royal nezer, a circlet rather than a full-blown crown) and the armband (royal jewelry) are exactly what a scavenger would have taken from a dead king. Then David took hold on his clothes, and rent them; and likewise all the men that were with him: And they mourned, and wept, and fasted until even, for Saul, and for Jonathan his son, and for the people of the LORD, and for the house of Israel; because they were fallen by the sword. Guidance: David's first response is grief, not opportunity. He tears his own clothes — the public sign of mourning the Amalekite had counterfeited on his way in. His men do the same. They mourn, weep, fast until evening. The chapter is showing us a David who, on the day his greatest enemy died, did not celebrate. The depth of his grief is exposed by what he is grieving for: 'Saul, and Jonathan his son, and the people of the LORD, and the house of Israel.' Four objects of grief. Two personal (Saul, Jonathan), two corporate (the LORD's people, the house of Israel). David is mourning his persecutor in the same breath as his beloved friend. The personal man who hunted him and the personal man who loved him are both in the dust on Gilboa, and David's tears are for both. The category 'people of the LORD' and 'house of Israel' broadens the grief beyond the personal — Israel itself has lost; many Israelites died; the kingdom has been hurt. David does not narrow his sorrow to what serves his political moment. The chapter's contrast with the Amalekite is total. The Amalekite stood on Gilboa and saw an opportunity; David stood at Ziklag and saw a tragedy. The character of the one who is about to reign is on display.

Then David took hold on his clothes, and rent them; and likewise all the men that were with him: And they mourned, and wept, and fasted until even, for Saul, and for Jonathan his son, and for the people of the LORD, and for the house of Israel; because they were fallen by the sword. Guidance: David's first response is grief, not opportunity. He tears his own clothes — the public sign of mourning the Amalekite had counterfeited on his way in. His men do the same. And David said unto the young man that told him, Whence art thou? And he answered, I am the son of a stranger, an Amalekite. And David said unto him, How wast thou not afraid to stretch forth thine hand to destroy the LORD's anointed? Guidance: After the period of mourning, David returns to the messenger. The question 'Whence art thou?' is a second look at the man's identity, this time judicially. The answer confirms it: 'I am the son of a stranger (ger — resident alien), an Amalekite.' His parents were Amalekite immigrants in Israelite territory; he had been functionally Israelite by residence but Amalekite by ethnicity, which is why he was traveling with the Israelite army. (Or he was a foreign mercenary in Saul's army; either reading fits the Hebrew.) Then David's accusation: 'How wast thou not afraid to stretch forth thine hand to destroy the LORD's anointed?' The very phrase David used about Saul to Abishai in the cave (1 Sam 24:6) and at Hachilah (1 Sam 26:9) — 'the LORD's anointed.' David has built his life around refusing to kill Saul. He is not going to make an exception for the man who claims to have done it for him. The theological category 'the LORD's anointed' applies to Saul even at the moment of Saul's death; the office did not lapse before the appointed successor inherits it. David's policy is consistent — he will not benefit politically from killing the LORD's anointed, and he will not let someone else benefit politically from it either.

And David said unto the young man that told him, Whence art thou? And he answered, I am the son of a stranger, an Amalekite. And David said unto him, How wast thou not afraid to stretch forth thine hand to destroy the LORD's anointed? Guidance: After the period of mourning, David returns to the messenger. The question 'Whence art thou?' is a second look at the man's identity, this time judicially. The answer confirms it: 'I am the son of a stranger (ger — resident alien), an Amalekite.' His parents were Amalekite immigrants in Israelite territory; he had been functionally Israelite by residence but Amalekite by ethnicity, which is why he was traveling with the Israelite army. (Or he was a foreign mercenary in Saul's army; either reading fits the Hebrew.) Then David's accusation: 'How wast thou not afraid to stretch forth thine hand to destroy the LORD's anointed?' The very phrase David used about Saul to Abishai in the cave (1 Sam 24:6) and at Hachilah (1 Sam 26:9) — 'the LORD's anointed.' David has built his life around refusing to kill Saul. He is not going to make an exception for the man who claims to have done it for him. And David called one of the young men, and said, Go near, and fall upon him. And he smote him that he died. And David said unto him, Thy blood be upon thy head; for thy mouth hath testified against thee, saying, I have slain the LORD's anointed. Guidance: The Amalekite is executed on his own testimony. David calls one of his men, gives the order; the verdict is carried out. Note the legal principle David uses: 'thy mouth hath testified against thee.' David does not investigate further — he does not even know whether the Amalekite is lying (which the narrator knows he is). The Amalekite's own admission is sufficient grounds. By his own words he confessed to murdering the LORD's anointed king. Whether the confession is true or false is, for the purposes of judgment, immaterial; the man has voluntarily condemned himself by his own claim, and David applies the consequence the claim deserves. The whole episode is a sustained warning: do not present yourself to power on the basis of a sin you think the powerful will reward. The man came to David expecting a paycheck; he received execution. The chapter is establishing the first major precedent of David's reign: no one will be honored for taking the life of the LORD's anointed, even when the LORD's anointed has fallen. The throne David is about to take will not be founded on the convenience of someone else's murder. The same principle will recur in 2 Sam 4 with the assassins of Ish-bosheth — David executes them too. The throne is being founded on a public, repeated, unmistakable disavowal of any blood-payment that brought the throne forward. There is a category of leadership in which the very first acts in office are the acts that define what the office will refuse to accept as gain.

And David lamented with this lamentation over Saul and over Jonathan his son: (Also he bade them teach the children of Judah the use of the bow: behold, it is written in the book of Jasher.) Guidance: After the execution, the lament. David composes one of the great elegies of the Old Testament. The narrator gives us two parentheticals before the poem itself. (1) 'Also he bade them teach the children of Judah the use of the bow' — the poem will be called the 'Song of the Bow' (qeshet), probably because of its reference to Jonathan's bow in v22; David made it required learning for Judah, a song every young Judahite must know. The political function of this is striking: David is teaching his future tribe a song that publicly mourns Saul, which can only have helped reconcile northern (Saulide) sympathies to David's eventual reign. (2) 'It is written in the book of Jasher' — the 'Book of the Upright,' an ancient Israelite anthology of poetry now lost. The chapter is preserving for us a poem that already existed as a separate document; the historian is quoting from a source. The whole arrangement is signaling that what follows is a literary masterpiece, not just a personal outburst. David is a poet, and he is using the form publicly. The lament will not be private grief; it will be a national song, taught by rote, sung as a tradition. He is shaping Israel's memory of Saul through the music itself.

And David lamented with this lamentation over Saul and over Jonathan his son: (Also he bade them teach the children of Judah the use of the bow: behold, it is written in the book of Jasher.) (1) 'Also he bade them teach the children of Judah the use of the bow' — the poem will be called the 'Song of the Bow' (qeshet), probably because of its reference to Jonathan's bow in v22; David made it required learning for Judah, a song every young Judahite must know. (2) 'It is written in the book of Jasher' — the 'Book of the Upright,' an ancient Israelite anthology of poetry now lost. The whole arrangement is signaling that what follows is a literary masterpiece, not just a personal outburst. David is a poet, and he is using the form publicly. The lament will not be private grief; it will be a national song, taught by rote, sung as a tradition. He is shaping Israel's memory of Saul through the music itself.

The beauty of Israel is slain upon thy high places: how are the mighty fallen! Guidance: The opening line of the lament. 'The beauty of Israel' (Hebrew tzevi yisrael) is a word that means 'gazelle / ornament / glory' — the most striking and graceful thing about Israel. Probably referring to Saul (the tall, handsome king) and Jonathan (the beloved prince) both — Israel's most physically beautiful royal pair. They were the kingdom's visible glory; now they are bodies on a ridge. 'Slain upon thy high places' — the very mountain (Gilboa) is addressed; the song apostrophizes the geography. 'How are the mighty fallen!' — eyk naphlu gibborim — the refrain that will recur three times in the poem (here, v25, v27). The Hebrew is a single elongated cry. The repetition is the heartbeat of the poem; David keeps coming back to the same phrase because he cannot get past the magnitude of the loss. Sometimes the most honest grief is a single line repeated until it becomes the bell that tolls every other thought.

Tell it not in Gath, publish it not in the streets of Askelon; lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice, lest the daughters of the uncircumcised triumph. Guidance: 'Tell it not in Gath' — the famous opening of the war-news prohibition. Gath and Ashkelon are the two largest Philistine cities; their female populations would have led the victory celebrations had the news traveled (cf. Miriam in Exod 15:20, the Israelite women in 1 Sam 18:6). The grief is so total that David cannot bear the thought of the enemy's joy. The line is rhetorical — of course the news would reach Gath and Ashkelon; the Philistines have already published it 'in the house of their idols' (1 Sam 31:9). But the poetic gesture is to refuse the spread; to wish, for one verse, that the news could be contained. The grief of God's people often takes the form of wishing the loss had been less public. There is a category of sorrow that resents not just the loss but the broadcast.

Ye mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew, neither let there be rain, upon you, nor fields of offerings: for there the shield of the mighty is vilely cast away, the shield of Saul, as though he had not been anointed with oil. Guidance: The curse on Gilboa. 'Let there be no dew, neither let there be rain, upon you, nor fields of offerings.' David curses the very mountain on which Saul fell — let it become barren, no dew (the chief moisture-source for Israeli agriculture in summer), no rain (in winter), no terumot ('offerings,' or grain produce). The image is the rocky ridge of Gilboa drying out into desolation as a kind of geographical witness to what was lost on it. (The actual Gilboa today is in fact quite barren on the upper slopes; some have read this as an oddly literal fulfillment of the curse. The biblical writer was probably noting an already-arid feature and theologizing it.) Then the specific grief: 'the shield of the mighty is vilely cast away, the shield of Saul.' The image is of Saul's anointed king-shield abandoned, unrecognized as the shield of an anointed king, treated as battle scrap. 'As though he had not been anointed with oil' — the very category David has been honoring his whole adult life. Even Saul's death cannot make Saul not-anointed, but on Gilboa his body and his shield were treated as though the anointing had never been. The line is a protest against the dishonor of the death-scene.

From the blood of the slain, from the fat of the mighty, the bow of Jonathan turned not back, and the sword of Saul returned not empty. Saul and Jonathan were lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided: they were swifter than eagles, they were stronger than lions. Guidance: A double tribute. (1) Their effectiveness as warriors: Jonathan's bow always found a target; Saul's sword always drew blood. The image of their weapons returning satiated — drinking blood and fat — is grim but ancient-warrior-honor language. They never went into battle and came back empty. (2) Their character together: 'lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided.' The line is one of the gentlest in the poem. David is naming the pair as a unit — the father who had failed and the son who had been beautiful — and praising their inseparability in life and death. Theologically the line is mostly accurate to character (Jonathan stayed with Saul to the end, even though he had given the kingdom to David); historically the line elides the spear-throwing and the slaughter of priests; but as a poem of mourning, it chooses to remember the best of the two together. David is shaping the public memory toward grace. Then the metaphor: 'swifter than eagles, stronger than lions.' Speed and strength — the qualities of a warrior. The animal imagery elevates the two from human kings to the apex predators of land and sky. It is the highest praise David can give.

From the blood of the slain, from the fat of the mighty, the bow of Jonathan turned not back, and the sword of Saul returned not empty. Saul and Jonathan were lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided: they were swifter than eagles, they were stronger than lions. Guidance: A double tribute. (1) Their effectiveness as warriors: Jonathan's bow always found a target; Saul's sword always drew blood. The image of their weapons returning satiated — drinking blood and fat — is grim but ancient-warrior-honor language. They never went into battle and came back empty. (2) Their character together: 'lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided.' The line is one of the gentlest in the poem. David is naming the pair as a unit — the father who had failed and the son who had been beautiful — and praising their inseparability in life and death. Theologically the line is mostly accurate to character (Jonathan stayed with Saul to the end, even though he had given the kingdom to David); historically the line elides the spear-throwing and the slaughter of priests; but as a poem of mourning, it chooses to remember the best of the two together. David is shaping the public memory toward grace. Then the metaphor: 'swifter than eagles, stronger than lions.' Speed and strength — the qualities of a warrior. The animal imagery elevates the two from human kings to the apex predators of land and sky. It is the highest praise David can give.

Ye daughters of Israel, weep over Saul, who clothed you in scarlet, with other delights, who put on ornaments of gold upon your apparel. Guidance: David addresses the women of Israel directly. 'Weep over Saul.' He calls them to mourn the king they had once celebrated. The line is striking because Israelite women had celebrated David at Saul's expense back in 18:6-7 ('Saul his thousands, David his ten thousands') — the song that triggered Saul's hatred. David is now calling those same women, who had elevated him, to weep for the king his rise had eclipsed. The reason: 'Saul clothed you in scarlet, with other delights, who put on ornaments of gold upon your apparel.' Saul's victories had produced spoil that became the luxury goods Israelite women enjoyed — scarlet-dyed cloth (a luxury fabric), gold ornaments. Saul's reign had economic benefits for the women; his wars had filled Israelite homes with finery that came from the defeated peoples. David is naming Saul's contribution, not erasing it. The reign that failed at the end also did real work along the way. Honest mourning does not pretend the failed leader did no good; it remembers the dyed cloth and the gold and lets the women weep over the king who had brought them.

How are the mighty fallen in the midst of the battle! O Jonathan, thou wast slain in thine high places. I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan: very pleasant hast thou been unto me: thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women. Guidance: The refrain returns — 'How are the mighty fallen!' — and the poem narrows from the pair to the friend. From this point the lament is for Jonathan alone, the personal grief inside the public grief. 'I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan.' 'Brother' (ah) — they were brothers-in-law (David married Michal, Jonathan's sister) but the term means more: covenant brother, soul-brother, the friend Jonathan had become. 'Very pleasant hast thou been unto me.' The verb na'amta is intimate; it describes the warmth a person finds in someone they delight in. Then the line that has been read in every direction for centuries: 'thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women.' Some modern readers have tried to make this a romantic claim. The Hebrew does not require it. 'The love of women' in ancient Near-Eastern poetic idiom is the deepest natural affection a man can imagine — the bond of wife or mother. David is saying that Jonathan's covenant love for him exceeded even that deepest natural bond. The love at issue is the covenant friendship of chapters 18-23, which gave the throne away and protected David through the wilderness years — a love that produced thrones-renounced and lives-risked, not romance. The verse is the highest tribute one Old Testament man pays to another: the friendship of Jonathan was rarer and deeper than the closest natural family bond. David has lost the friend who told him 'thou shalt be king and I shall be next' (1 Sam 23:17) and never lived to be next. The grief is total.

How are the mighty fallen in the midst of the battle! O Jonathan, thou wast slain in thine high places. I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan: very pleasant hast thou been unto me: thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women. Guidance: The refrain returns — 'How are the mighty fallen!' — and the poem narrows from the pair to the friend. From this point the lament is for Jonathan alone, the personal grief inside the public grief. 'I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan.' 'Brother' (ah) — they were brothers-in-law (David married Michal, Jonathan's sister) but the term means more: covenant brother, soul-brother, the friend Jonathan had become. 'Very pleasant hast thou been unto me.' The verb na'amta is intimate; it describes the warmth a person finds in someone they delight in. Then the line that has been read in every direction for centuries: 'thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women.' Some modern readers have tried to make this a romantic claim. The Hebrew does not require it. 'The love of women' in ancient Near-Eastern poetic idiom is the deepest natural affection a man can imagine — the bond of wife or mother. David is saying that Jonathan's covenant love for him exceeded even that deepest natural bond. The love at issue is the covenant friendship of chapters 18-23, which gave the throne away and protected David through the wilderness years — a love that produced thrones-renounced and lives-risked, not romance. The verse is the highest tribute one Old Testament man pays to another: the friendship of Jonathan was rarer and deeper than the closest natural family bond. David has lost the friend who told him 'thou shalt be king and I shall be next' (1 Sam 23:17) and never lived to be next. The grief is total.

How are the mighty fallen, and the weapons of war perished! Guidance: The poem ends as it began. 'How are the mighty fallen' — the refrain for the third time. 'And the weapons of war perished' — closing image, the broken-and-cast-away shields and bows and swords of Gilboa, the equipment that will never lift again. The end of the poem is also the end of an era. The first king of Israel and his crown prince are dead. The shield that David sang about in v21 is lying on a ridge in the north. The bow that fed Jonathan's arrows in v22 is silent in the dust. Israel is leaderless. The chapter — and the official mourning for Saul — ends on the closing word of the lament. Tomorrow, in chapter 2, David will inquire of the LORD whether to go up to a city of Judah, the LORD will tell him to go to Hebron, and the men of Judah will anoint him king. The kingdom transfer begins after the lament, not during it. David does not lift a finger toward the throne until he has thoroughly buried his predecessor in song. Sometimes the most important political work a leader does is the grief he honors before he reaches for the next office.
